title: Re-Select — {key:gv}
subtitle: Bring back the last visual selection.
summary: gv re-enters *visual mode* with the same selection you last had — same shape, same endpoints. Essential for re-applying an operator to the same region.

blocks.text: {key:g}{key:v} re-enters *visual mode* with the *exact same selection* you last had — same shape, same lines, same characters.

blocks.title: Where the Selection Is Stored
blocks.text:
{key:g}{key:v} reads the special marks {key:`}{key:<} and {key:`}{key:>} (start and end of last visual) plus an internal record of the *visual mode shape* (character/line/block) that was active. Together those three things are enough to recreate any visual selection.

The selection persists across:

  • Mode changes (Normal/Insert/Ex)
  • Cursor moves
  • Most edits — though if your edit *removes* the selected text, gv may select an empty or unexpected region.

{key:g}{key:v} doesn't survive `:e` (re-edit). The marks are buffer-local: each buffer has its own last-visual.
After a {key::}{key:s} substitution that you ran on a visual selection, {key:g}{key:v} brings the (modified) selection back so you can re-substitute or re-indent.

blocks.title: {key:g}{key:v} reads the {key:'}{key:<} / {key:'}{key:>} marks
blocks.text: The last visual selection is persisted into two special marks ({key:'}{key:<} for start, {key:'}{key:>} for end) plus a stored mode tag (charwise/linewise/blockwise). {key:g}{key:v} simply re-enters *visual mode* using those three pieces of state. The marks survive across commands, which is why you can edit, jump around, then come back with {key:g}{key:v} and find your old selection intact.
